➥ The fight over prediction markets in the US is getting messy

a16z just stepped in, backing the idea that state by state rules are breaking the system.

Here’s the core issue:

→ some states are trying to block or restrict prediction market access
→ others allow it
→ result = fragmented access depending on where you live

a16z’s argument is simple:

Forcing platforms to block users based on location goes against the idea of fair and equal market access.

And it creates a bigger problem: less liquidity , more friction and weaker markets overall  

At the same time , the CFTC says these markets fall under federal jurisdiction but states argue they look like gambling and should be regulated locally  

This isn’t just about one product.

It’s about whether financial markets can stay open and consistent across the country or become fragmented by geography.

And how that gets resolved will shape how new digital markets grow in the US.

kept.text:
Good morning Legendary builders and Believers

The seven-month streak where leading prediction markets Kalshi and Polymarket’s combined trading volume set a fresh record monthly high ended in April, according to The Block’s data.

Last month saw the first fall-off in total sector trading activity since the platforms started seeing exploding growth in September.
That said, the decline was driven by lower activity on Polymarket's global platform, while the firm's U.S. subsidiary, which is still being rolled out, grew, as did Kalshi.

The number of active traders of Polymarket dropped to about 643,000 in April, down from over 733,000 in March, breaking another seven-month growth streak, according to The Block’s data. This coincided with a drop in U.S. dollar volumes on the site, as well as a plunge in notional volume on both Polymarket and Kalshi.
Still, April saw Polymarket and Kalshi's combined lifetime volumes cross $150 billion.

Polymarket and Kalshi remain the leading betting markets in a sector that is beginning to gain widespread attention and scrutiny. Kalshi, in particular, is quickly gaining traction in the sports betting category, which is drawing the ire of state-level regulators and gaming authorities.

Kalshi won a legal battle against the Commodity Futures Trading Commission in 2024 to offer election-related contracts, opening the door for it to expand and gain a foothold in the U.S. That lawsuit also paved the way for Polymarket to reenter the U.S. by acquiring a CFTC-licensed derivatives exchange, after it was banned for offering unregistered event contracts. Polymarket is now reportedly looking to onboard its entire global marketplace into the U.S.

The CFTC, under a new administration, now embraces prediction market experimentation, having issued guidance to foster innovation, withdrawing prior stricter guidelines on gaming contracts, and having filed lawsuits against several states that are looking to bar prediction markets based on the legal theory that events contracts are binary derivatives under federal remit.

In March, Kalshi raised capital at a $22 billion valuation. Polymarket, previously backed by NYSE parent Intercontinental Exchange, is reportedly looking to raise at a $15 billion valuation. Both firms are actively taking steps to bar insider trading on their platforms.

kept.text:
Prediction markets are no longer a niche. They are becoming one of the most profitable primitives in crypto right now, and one setup stands out above everything else.

April made that very clear.

@Polymarket generated ~$43.36M in protocol fees in April, effectively doubling its March performance and pushing the platform to an estimated ~$520M annualized run rate. At that level, it’s already approaching the revenue profile of peak-cycle DeFi leaders like @Uniswap, but with a completely different core engine: monetizing information instead of liquidity.

What makes this especially important is how that revenue is achieved. Polymarket operates without a token, without incentives, and without any points farming mechanics. The platform processed roughly $2.17B in monthly volume, driven purely by organic demand for pricing real-world probabilities. In a market still dominated by emissions and artificial growth loops, this is one of the clearest signals of real product-market fit, where users are not incentivized to participate, but are actively paying to express a view on the future.

At the same time, competition is accelerating across completely different architectures. Hyperliquid rolled out prediction markets via HIP-4 and reportedly matched Polymarket-level activity within hours on initial markets, showing how quickly liquidity can move when execution is strong. On the regulated side, Kalshi continues scaling through fiat rails and institutional access, even integrating Bitcoin as a major payment method.

This creates a rare situation where three parallel models are expanding at once: a crypto-native system (@Polymarket), a high-performance perp-integrated stack (@HyperliquidX), and a regulated TradFi platform (@Kalshi). All three are converging on the same core idea: turning uncertainty into a tradable asset class, but Polymarket already sits at the center of that shift by capturing the demand layer rather than just facilitating execution.

And this is where the asymmetry becomes obvious.

Polymarket is already generating hundreds of millions in annualized fees with no token attached. No governance premium, no speculation premium, and no narrative premium are priced in yet, which means what exists today is simply raw, unpriced fundamentals.

Historically, this is exactly the stage where tokenization happens, not to create demand, but to capture it. Even conservative valuation frameworks suggest that a 10–20× price-to-fee multiple would place it in the $5B–$10B+ FDV range on day one.

That’s why the real takeaway is not just the growth.

It’s this:

Polymarket today is probably the most obvious token launch setup in crypto right now.

kept.text:
A16Z BACKS CFTC IN STATE PREDICTION MARKET BAN FIGHT

@a16z filed an 18-page letter to the CFTC Friday, siding with the federal regulator against state-level crackdowns on @Kalshi, @Polymarket, and other prediction market platforms.

The CFTC has sued Illinois, Arizona, Connecticut, New York, and Wisconsin in the past month for trying to regulate event contracts that fall under federal jurisdiction. State AGs argue these are unlicensed gambling. CFTC Chair Mike Selig says they're swaps.

Polymarket and Kalshi cumulative volume crossed $150B in April. The fight over who actually regulates prediction markets just became one of the biggest jurisdictional battles in crypto.

kept.text:
Most prediction markets in 2026 still inherit the same structural flaw.
Platforms like Polymarket or Kalshi depend on external oracles for data and rely on token voting, curators or dispute mechanisms to settle outcomes. Even clear oracle reports can lead to interpretation battles, delays and governance overhead.
The resolution layer remains social and fragile.
Flashcast_social is built differently.
You can create a market in multiple ways. The simplest is quoting or replying to any post on X. Rules and winning conditions are cryptographically locked at creation through immutable smart contracts and ZK commitments. No one can alter them later.
Resolution is automatic. It uses data oracles and is verified by Fermah Froben, the universal proof market. The entire process is deterministic and provable on chain.
No committees. No post event disputes. No governance risk after launch.
This turns prediction markets from governance dependent systems into protocol level truth settlement.
While others manage disputes after the fact, Flashcast prevents them by architecture.
Even in private beta the difference is noticeable.
What do you see as the biggest unresolved challenge in prediction market design today?
